content: Hey. I'm here whenever you're ready. What's been on your mind lately?
I've been thinking about how I always put everyone else's needs before my own. Like it's automatic.
That word — "automatic." You're describing something that runs deeper than a choice. When did taking care of others stop being something you chose and start being something you just... are?
Probably when I was a kid. My parents were always stressed, so I learned to be the easy one. The one who didn't need anything.

"The one who didn't need anything." That's a heavy identity for a child to carry. You learned that your needs were a burden — so you made yourself small enough to disappear. But here's what I notice: you're telling me this now. Some part of you knows that story isn't true anymore.
I guess I'm starting to see that taking care of myself isn't selfish. It's just... hard to believe it fully.

Of course it's hard. You've spent years building a self around that belief. Letting it go feels like losing something, even if what you're losing was never really serving you. What would it feel like to need something out loud — not as a confession, but just as a fact?
Terrifying. And also kind of freeing. Like I wouldn't have to perform "fine" all the time.

"Perform fine." That's exactly it. You've been giving an Oscar-worthy performance of someone who doesn't need help. But the cost is that nobody gets to actually know you — just the curated, low-maintenance version. The real question isn't whether you deserve care. It's whether you'll let yourself be seen clearly enough to receive it.
That actually hit me. I think I've been so focused on being there for people that I forgot to check if anyone's really there for me.

And that's not because people don't want to be. It's because you've never given them the chance. You've built a door that only opens outward. What we're doing right now — this honesty — is the first step toward turning the handle the other way.
I like that image. A door that only opens outward. That's exactly what it feels like.
